text: The surgeon uses a sponge to keep the small bowel out of the surgical field and begins a medial to lateral dissection for the right colectomy.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection within the avascular plane, carefully avoiding the ureter. As the dissection progresses cephalad, the duodenum becomes visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicles at their origin near the superior mesenteric vein, visible as a blue structure.
The surgeon isolates the vein at its junction with the superior mesenteric vein, clearing surrounding lymphatic tissue. The vessel is then divided using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon targets the artery, visible just beneath the previously isolated vein, ensuring a clear circumferential view by sweeping down the duodenum.

The surgeon dissects around the ileocolic artery and uses a vessel sealer to divide it. The dissection follows the medial plane, exposing the pancreas and proceeding along the superior mesenteric vessels. The absence of a right colic artery is noted.
The surgeon continues the dissection cephalad, revealing the middle colic vessels. The long middle colic trunk is traced, and its origin is visible.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the right branch of the middle colic vessels, revealing the bifurcation. Scissors are used for precise dissection, as the vessel sealer is deemed inadequate for fine dissection. A vein is visible, possibly a branch of the loop of Henle or the right colic vein, located in the expected vicinity.
The surgeon continues to dissect and isolate the right branch of the middle colic vessels, which are visibly coming into view.

The surgeon dissects around the right branch of the middle colic vein, carefully removing tension before dividing it. The right branch of the middle colic artery is also identified and prepared for division.

The surgeon advances along the transverse colon mesentery towards the planned margin, encountering marginal vessels while ensuring adequate lymphatic clearance from previously taken vessels at the root origin.

The surgeon splits the omentum on the cephalad surface of the specimen, entering the vascular plane at the junction with the colon. After splitting the omentum, the hepatic flexure is mobilized by dissecting over the top.
The surgeon continues the dissection around the hepatic flexure, having already navigated the plane above the gastrocolic ligament, facilitating easier access to the area.

The surgeon completes a medial dissection extending laterally, encountering minimal tissue tethering. As the dissection progresses inferiorly towards the terminal ileum and appendix, the ability to continue from a cephalad direction diminishes.

The patient is positioned in a slight Trendelenburg, and the surgeon approaches from an inferior angle to separate the terminal ileum (TI) mesentery from its origin, ensuring the ureter is identified and preserved during the dissection.

The surgeon completes the dissection by meeting the medial plane, ensuring the terminal ileum (TI) and entire colon are completely free. The small bowel mesentery is left for last, as the TI can be folded on itself before full lateral mobilization. The surgeon checks perfusion to the margins.
The surgeon marks the colon with cautery for visibility under white light, emphasizing the colon side over the small bowel side. Stapling is performed from the suprapubic port.
The surgeon positions the specimen in the pelvis and aligns the small bowel with the colon, ensuring the mesentery is untwisted. A colotomy and enterotomy are performed through the suprapubic port.

The surgeon adjusts the angle of the surgical arm to ensure the stapler can be effectively introduced through the suprapubic port. The stapler is used to create an anastomosis, with the white load selected to minimize bleeding.

The surgeon sutures the common channel closed after creating the anastomosis, starting at the near corner with a running baseball stitch around the bottom corner, transitioning to a baseball stitch up to the top part of the common channel enterotomy.

The surgeon cinches the suture tightly to guide it precisely for optimal closure of the common channel. Using a three or six-inch V-lock suture, the surgeon imbricates the tissue, ensuring the enterotomy remains a reasonable size to prevent stretching or tearing during stapler placement. The final step involves covering the area with omentum before undocking and extracting through the suprapubic port.
